result.markdown:
--- title: "Questionnaire" bibliography: references.bib echo: false --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.callout-caution} This is a draft for the questionnaire. Please make suggestions to remove/add variables, or to adapt current items. ::: # Consent form ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: You are invited to participate in a research study that will ask about your opinions and attitudes. You must be at least 18 years of age to participate. There are no risks associated with this study and your identity will be kept confidential. We cannot and do not guarantee or promise that you will receive any benefits from this study. Participation If you decide to participate in this project, please understand your participation is voluntary and you may withdraw your consent or discontinue participation at any time without penalty. The alternative is not to participate. You have the right to refuse to answer particular questions. Your individual privacy will be maintained in all published and written data resulting from the study. Contact Information If you have any questions, concerns or complaints about this research, its procedures, risks and benefits, contact us at trustclimsci.megastudy@gmail.com. If you agree to participate in this research, please click to the next screen and complete the questionnaire. # Attention checks/LLM detection ::: {.callout-caution} There are several checks in here---too many. The idea is to make a selection. ::: ## Intro ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: You will need to qualify for this study. You will find out if you qualify shortly. This study takes about 12-15 min to complete. It has several sections requiring attention. Because participating until the very end of this study and answering all questions is the only way we can use your responses, it is very important to us that you help and answer the following questions honestly. Do you agree to pay attention and participate in all sections of this study? [Yes, No] ## Basic check ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: To help us keep track of who is paying attention, please select "Somewhat disagree" in the options below. [Strongly agree; Agree; Somewhat agree; Neither agree nor disagree; Somewhat disagree; Disagree; Strongly disagree] ## Reading comprehension ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Please read the following short article. Officials in a midsize town have been working for four years on a plan to produce an event license to cover all of the major events that occur at the town’s local stadium, which hosts concerts and home sports games. The application would be submitted each January and list all events expected to occur at the stadium over the next 12 months. If an unlisted event emerges during the year, lawmakers could hold a special hearing on the event, or accept it without a hearing and add it into the existing license. To assist with this plan, lawmakers filed legislation that would change state licensing laws so that annual event licenses will expire within one year. “This makes a minor change to current law, which provides that all licenses issued shall expire on December 31 of each year,” a lawmaker said. What was the topic of the short article you just read about? [Medical funding; Event licensing; Political polarization; City budgeting; Election monitoring policy; Campaign finance reform] ## Transition ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Congratulations, you have qualified for the full study! As you proceed to the next section, please make sure you do not close out of this tab. You must complete the whole study to collect your payment. I understand I must complete the full study to collect my full earnings. # Pre-treatment ## Demographics ### Gender What is your gender? [Male; Female; Other [text box]] ### Age How old are you? ### Education ::: {.callout-note} From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: What is the highest degree or level of school you have completed? [Less than high school graduate; High school graduate, diploma or the equivalent (for example: GED); Some college credit, no degree; Trade/technical/vocational training; Associate degree; Bachelor's degree; Master's degree; Professional degree; Doctorate degree] **Alternative** ::: {.callout-note} From @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025 ::: What is your highest completed level of education? [Did not attend school; Primary education; Secondary education (e.g., high school); Higher education (e.g., university degree or higher education diploma)] ### Religiosity and Religious affiliation/denomination ### Income ::: {.callout-note} From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Which of the following ranges includes your total annual household income from all sources? [Less than \$25,000; \$25,000 to \$34,999; \$35,000 to \$49,999; \$50,000 to \$74,999; \$75,000 to \$99,999; \$100,000 to \$124,999; \$125,000 to \$149,999; \$150,000 to \$174,999; \$175,000 to \$199,999; \$200,000 to \$249,999; \$250,000 or more] ### Urban rural ### Race ::: {.callout-note} From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: Please select which race / ethnicity you identify as. (Please select all that apply.) [White / Caucasian; Black / African American; Hispanic / Latino; Asian / Asian American; Other [text box]] ### Geographical location (postcode) ::: {.callout-note} This could help us measure physical distance to science. We could, for example, assess the density of scientific institutions in a given postcode area. Or maybe, an even better measure would be measuring an average distance of a postcode to **all** major scientific institutions in the US. Will probably highly correlate with urban vs. rural measures, but could allow for interesting comparisons within urban areas. ::: What is your zip code? [text box] ### Conservatism ::: {.callout-note} From @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: In general, do you think of yourself as ... [Extremely liberal; Liberal; Slightly liberal; Moderate, middle of the road; Slightly conservative; Conservative; Extremely conservative] ## Moderator varaibles ### Partisan identity ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @voelkelMegastudyTesting252024 ::: #### Party Generally speaking, do you usually think of yourself as a Republican, a Democrat, an Independent, or other? [Republican; Democrat; Independent; Other [text box]] #### Intesity How important is being a [Republican/Democrat/Independent/Other] to you? Below is a range from 0 to 100 indicating how important this is to you. Click on any space within this range and a bar will appear. Feel free to move that bar around to the number that best represents your answer. [Slider, 0 = "Not important at all" to 1 0 "Extremely important"] ### Social dominance orientation/authoritarianism/system justification ::: {.callout-caution} tbd ::: ### Perceived social status and/or perceived inequality ::: {.callout-caution} tbd ::: ### Trust in institutions (US context) ::: {.callout-caution} We might want to use a Slider here, too? ::: From the US General Social Survey (GSS): "I am going to name some institutions in this country. As far as the people running these institutions are concerned, would you say you have a great deal of confidence, only some confidence, or hardly any confidence at all in them?" - "Banks and financial institutions" - "Major companies" - "Organized religion" - "Education" - "Executive branch of the federal government" - "Organized Labor" - "Press" - "Medicine" - "TV" - "U.S. Supreme Court" - "Scientific community" - "Congress" - "Military" ### Psychological distance to science ::: {.callout-caution} check Bastiaan's studies ::: - how many scientists do you know - how often do you visit, say, museums # Interventions ::: {.callout-caution} tbd ::: ## Transition You are now moving on to a different section of the study. Please answer the following questions to the best of your ability. Thank you. # Post-treatment ## Main coutcome ### Trust in climate scientists ::: {#tbl-trust .cell tbl-cap='Multi-dimensional measure of trust in climate scientists. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Dimension Item Response Options
We are interested in your general opinion about climate scientists. In answering the following questions, think about climate scientists specifically in the context of their work/research.
Competence How incompetent or competent are most climate scientists? 0 = Very incompetent … 100 = Very competent
How unintelligent or intelligent are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unintelligent … 100 = Very intelligent
How unqualified or qualified are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unqualified … 100 = Very qualified
Integrity How dishonest or honest are most climate scientists? 0 = Very dishonest … 100 = Very honest
How unethical or ethical are most climate scientists? 0 = Very unethical … 100 = Very ethical
How insincere or sincere are most climate scientists? 0 = Very insincere … 100 = Very sincere
Benevolence How unconcerned or concerned are most climate scientists about people’s wellbeing? 0 = Very unconcerned … 100 = Very concerned
How uneager or eager are most climate scientists to improve others’ lives? 0 = Very uneager … 100 = Very eager
How inconsiderate or considerate are most climate scientists of others’ interests? 0 = Very inconsiderate … 100 = Very considerate
Openness How open, if at all, are most climate scientists to feedback? 0 = Not open … 100 = Very open
How unwilling or willing are most climate scientists to be transparent? 0 = Very unwilling … 100 = Very willing
How little or much attention do climate scientists pay to others' views? 0 = Very little attention … 100 = A great deal of attention
``` ::: ::: ## Secondary outcomes ### Behavioral measures #### Donation ::: {.callout-note} To assess consequential and impactful behavior, participants will be given the option to donate a monetary bonus to the American Geophysical Union (AGU), or keep it for themselves. The AGU was chosen because it is the leading scientific association for scientists working on climate change. ::: For the next question, we would like you to have the opportunity to donate money to a non-profit or to keep it for yourself. Please decide how you would like to allocate \$10 between yourself and the American Geophysical Union (AGU). The AGU is a non-profit, non-partisan scientific association. It is the largest scientific association in the area of climate change in the US, with over 15,000 scientist members. You can give all of the money to the AGU and keep none for yourself, or you can keep all the money for yourself and give none to AGU, or you can pick any split in between. Note: We will randomly select a subset of 100 participants and realize their choices. [Slider, 0\$ to 10\$] ### Scientists' role in policy making ::: {.callout-note} Scales were adapted from the "Normative perceptions of science and society" scale in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but slightly modified: We removed two items, one because it was not strictly true ("Climate scientists should remain independent from the policy-making process") and the other because it was not policy related ("Climate scientists should communicate their findings to the general public"). We also use sliders instead of a 5-point likert scale. ::: ::: {#tbl-policy-role .cell tbl-cap='Scientists\' role in policy making. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
To what extent do you agree or disagree with the following statements?
Climate scientists should work closely with politicians to integrate scientific results into policy-making 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should actively advocate for specific policies 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should communicate their findings to politicians 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
Climate scientists should be more involved in the policy-making process 0 = Strongly disagree … 100 = Strongly agree
````` ::: ::: ### Reliance on scientists for lifestyle choices ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from the "Willingness to be vulnerable to scientists" scale in @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but heavily modified. We retain only one item, as the other two ("How much or little should governments rely on climate scientists’ guidance when making decisions related to climate science?" and "How much or little control do you want climate scientists to have on government decisions related to science?") were conceptually closer to scientists' role in policy making. We also make changes to the one item we retain (original: "How willing are you to rely on climate scientists’ advice when making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition)? (1) Not at all, (2) A little, (3) A moderate amount, (4) A lot, (5) A great deal"), as the endpoints of the original likert scale might be asymmetric. Our modification also aligns better with our scale for measuring scientists' role in policy making. ::: To what extent do you agree or disagree with the following statement? "When making lifestyle choices (e.g., sustainable transportation, energy use and nutrition), I am willing to rely on climate scientists’ advice." [Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = Strongly agree] ### Single item trust in climate scientists ::: {.callout-note} We add a single item question on trust in climate scientists, to compare how this maps onto the different dimensions of our multi-dimension main outcome measure. ::: "How much do you trust climate scientists?" [Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = very strongly] ### Single item distrust in climate scientists ::: {.callout-note} We add an item on distrust and comparing it to the trust item will allow us to test if they are symmetrical or not. ::: "How much do you **distrust** climate scientists?" [Slider, 0 = not at all … 100 = very strongly] ### Support for climate policies ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, who used a modified version from @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. We use sliders instead of the original 3-point scale. I personally think it wouldbe nice to use as many items as possible from the more extensive scale from @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024: Climate change policy support. This dependent variable consisted of participants’ level of agreement from 0 = Not at all to 100 = Very much so using a slider (participants could also respond with “not applicable”, which is coded as “NA” in the dataset), with the following nine statements: “I support raising carbon taxes on gas/fossil fuels/coal,” “I support significantly expanding infrastructure for public transportation,” “I support increasing the number of charging stations for electric vehicles,” “I support increasing the use of sustainable energy such as wind and solar energy,” “I support increasing taxes on airline companies to offset carbon emissions,” “I support protecting forested and land areas,” “I support investing more in green jobs and businesses,” “I support introducing laws to keep waterways and oceans clean,” and “I support increasing taxes on carbon-intensive foods (for example, meat and dairy).” ::: ::: {#tbl-policy-support .cell tbl-cap='Support for climate policies. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How much do you support the following policies.
Raising carbon taxes on fossil fuels (e.g., gas, oil, coal) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Expanding infrastructure for public transportation 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing the use of sustainable energy such as wind and solar energy 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Protecting forested and land areas 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
Increasing taxes on carbon intense foods (e.g., beef and dairy products) 0 = not at all … 100 = Very strongly
````` ::: ::: ### Funding for research on climate change ::: {.callout-note} Adapted from the National [Science Foundation (NSF) – Science & Engineering Indicators](https://ncses.nsf.gov/pubs/nsb20227/public-perceptions-of-science-and-technology#general-perceptions-of-s-t), also used in @ophirFactorsAssessingSciences2023. ::: "Climate change research that advances the frontiers of knowledge is necessary and should be supported by funding from the federal government." [Slider, 0 = Strongly disagree, 100 = Strongly agree] ### Belief in climate change ::: {.callout-note} From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. ::: How accurate do you think this statement is? "Human activities are causing climate change." [Slider, 0 = not at all accurate, 100 = extremely accurate] ### Belief in climate crisis ::: {.callout-note} From @vlasceanuAddressingClimateChange2024. ::: ::: {#tbl-belief-crisis .cell tbl-cap='Belief in climate crisis. Answers are assessed with a slider from 0 to 100. Only the endpoints are labelled. By default, sliders are at scale midpoint.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Item Response Options
How accurate do you think these statements are?
Taking action to fight climate change is necessary to avoid a global catastrophe 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change poses a serious threat to humanity 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
Climate change is a global emergency 0 = Not at all accurate … 100 = Extremely accurate
````` ::: ::: ### Trust in specific scientific institutions ::: {.callout-caution} tbd For example, how much do you trust... - Environmental Protection Agency - Universities ::: ### Perceived personal benefits of science Adapted from @colognaTrustScientistsTheir2025, but we only rely on one item, and use a slider instead of a Likert scale: "In your opinion, how much does scientific research benefit people like yourself in your country?" [Slider, 0 = not at all, 100 = very strongly] ### Importance of trusting climate scientists [Open-ended question] Do you think trusting climate scientists is important for society? Please share your thoughts and arguments on why or why not.
